history: The client has a longstanding pattern of self-critical thinking and is used to maintaining control and achieving goals. This tendency has shaped their approach to challenges, making them sensitive to perceived failures or setbacks, especially in areas where they have traditionally excelled. Current stressors include job seeking, personal relationships, and family dynamics, with a notable conflict arising from another person's (Ivan's) approach to job searching and life decisions.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If others don't approach things the way I do, it means my way isn't good enough. I must maintain control and achieve to prove my worth. If I let go or accept different approaches, things will fall apart.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't control the situation or help others succeed, it means I'm failing and things will never get better.
coping_strategies: The client tends to ruminate on conflicts and perceived failures, especially in relation to job seeking and family issues. They may withdraw from discussions with Ivan or seek reassurance from others to validate their own approach, maintaining a cycle of self-criticism and avoidance.
situation: Conflict related to another person's (Ivan's) approach to job searching and life decisions.
auto_thought: Ivan is doing it all wrong; if he keeps this up, everything will fall apart and it's my fault for not fixing it.
behavior: I withdraw from the conversation with Ivan or try to control the discussion. I ruminate about what went wrong and seek reassurance from others about my approach.
